figures.caption: Table 1: Solution to (P̆) for different choice of tolerance distance ε and budget x0. The budget and α-BW column indicate whether the respective constraints are binding. Recall that εmin and ε∞ depend on x0, and that x∞0 depends on ε.

Table 3: Various summary statistics of the benchmark’s terminal wealth and the optimal strategy’s terminal wealth for varying p ∈ {1.6, 2, 2.4} and α ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 0.9}. Common parameters are c = 0.9, x0 = 1, γ = 1 2 , and ε = 0.5.

Figure 4: Optimal density function g∗ of (P̆) when both constraints are binding for different choices of α ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 0.9}. Left p = 1.6, middle p = 2, right p = 2.4. To the left of the red dashed vertical line, the strategies underperform the benchmark, i.e., F̆Y(·) > Ğη∗,α(·). To the right of the red dashed vertical line, the strategies outperform the benchmark. The red lines are numerically same for Ğη∗,α, α ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 0.9} and fixed p. Common parameters are c = 0.9, x0 = 1, γ = 1 2 , and ε = 0.5.

Figure 3: Optimal density function g∗ of (P̆) when both constraints are binding for different choices of p ∈ {1.6, 1.8, 2, 2.2, 2.4} of the Power Bregman generator. Left α = 0.1, right α = 0.5. Common parameters are x0 = 1, c = 0.9, γ = 1 2 , and ε = 0.5.

Figure 5: Optimal density function g∗ of (P̆) when both constraints are binding for different choices of c. Left c ∈ {0.5, 0.7, 0.9} and x0 = 1. Right (c, x0) ∈ {(0.8, 0.9), (0.85, 0.95), (0.9, 1)}. Common parameters are α = 0.1, p = 1.6, γ = 1 2 , and ε = 0.5.

Figure 1: Integrand of the α-BW divergence for the Power family ϕp from the quantile function Ğ given in (8) to the benchmark quantile F̆XπT . Top left panel: p ∈ {1.6, 1.8, 2, 2.2, 2.4} and α = 0.5. Top right panel: α ∈ {0.01, 0.25, 0.5} and p = 1.6. Bottom left panel: α ∈ {0.01, 0.25, 0.5} and p = 2. Bottom right panel: α ∈ {0.01, 0.25, 0.5} and p = 2.4.
Table 2: Constraints for different quantile function for the same parameters as in Figure 2. The benchmarks expected utility is 1.587, its cost is 1, and BWα(F̆Y , F̆Y) = 0.

Figure 2: Optimal density functions for the Power Bregman generator ϕp. Left: p = 2 (one-half the squared Wasserstein distance). Right: p = 1.6,. Common parameters α = 0.25, x0 = 1, c = 0.9, γ = 1 2 , and ε = 1 2 .
